sub_sections.title: Objectives and ambition
sub_sections.content:
MAESTRO — Multi-Agent Ecosystem for Strategic Task Reasoning and Orchestration — addresses a central and unresolved gap in AI agent research: existing approaches remain brittle when confronted with the open-ended complexity of real-world tasks. Current LLM-based agents, exemplified by ReAct and AutoGPT-style architectures, rely on chain-of-thought prompting and simple action loops that degrade unpredictably over long task horizons, lose coherence over extended interactions, and lack principled mechanisms for coordinating with other agents or integrating external tools and APIs into their reasoning and action loops. Classical planning and multi-agent systems provide formal guarantees but cannot handle the ambiguity and open-endedness of natural language task specifications. Memory-augmented neural systems focus on retrieval augmentation for single-turn accuracy without integration with planning or multi-agent coordination. These three research communities operate in largely disconnected tracks, preventing the emergence of AI agents that are simultaneously robust, trustworthy, and deployable in safety-critical and high-value sectors of the European economy.

MAESTRO's central thesis is that three foundational capabilities — structured planning with verifiable reasoning, persistent and adaptive memory, and decentralised multi-agent coordination — must be co-designed with a principled External Tool and API Orchestration Layer into a unified architecture. This integration is not achievable by combining off-the-shelf components from the three communities. It is the project's primary scientific and technical differentiator and its most important contribution to the state of the art in AI agent research.

The project is directly pertinent to the scope of topic HORIZON-CL4-2026-05-DIGITAL-EMERGING-02, which calls for research on next-generation AI agents with advances in planning, reasoning, memory management, and multi-agent coordination, and mandates application in at least one Apply AI sector. MAESTRO addresses all four mandatory scope areas — robust planning and reasoning (SR-02), memory and state management (SR-03), external tool and API integration (SR-04), and advanced multi-agent frameworks (SR-05, area (c)), in addition to enhanced AI agent autonomy (SR-05, area (a)) and memory-augmented AI for long-term reasoning (SR-05, area (b)) — as an integrated research programme. The project validates in two of the three call-identified Apply AI sectors (healthcare and advanced manufacturing) and in a cross-sector logistics and supply chain transfer demonstrator that confirms architectural portability beyond the minimum sector requirement.

MAESTRO pursues seven concrete, measurable objectives:

OBJ-1 — Neuro-symbolic planning engine (lead: Alpenstadt Technical University, ATU): Develop a hybrid neuro-symbolic planning engine that decomposes high-level natural language goals into executable sub-task graphs with formally specified pre-conditions, post-conditions, and verification checkpoints, integrating Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and hierarchical task network (HTN) planning with LLM-driven heuristic evaluation and plan-monitoring self-correction. Measurable target: ≥40% improvement in plan success rate over pure LLM-based baselines (ReAct, Reflexion) on standardised benchmarks (WebArena, SWE-bench, GAIA) for tasks requiring more than 10 sequential steps, by month 36.

OBJ-2 — Unified adaptive memory architecture (lead: Breda Institute for Intelligent Systems, BIIS): Design and implement a tri-store memory architecture integrating working memory, episodic memory, and semantic memory with a learned memory controller that manages information flow between stores. Measurable target: ≥30% improvement in task coherence and factual consistency over context-window-only baselines in interaction sequences exceeding 50 agent steps on MAESTRO's long-horizon evaluation suite, by month 30.

OBJ-3 — Decentralised multi-agent coordination protocols (lead: Centre Européen de Recherche en Informatique Avancée, CERIA): Develop formally specified task-delegation and negotiation protocols enabling heterogeneous AI agents to coordinate on shared objectives with provable convergence guarantees and auditable interaction traces. Measurable target: Effective coordination of ≥5 heterogeneous agents with ≥25% improvement in joint task completion over independent agent baselines and formal proof of protocol convergence, by month 36.

OBJ-8 — External Tool and API Orchestration Layer (lead: ATU): Develop a cross-cutting typed tool registry, formal tool selection and invocation policies, standardised API adapters (REST, GraphQL, HL7 FHIR, OPC-UA), post-invocation verification, execution monitoring with fallback/recovery, and auditable tool execution traces integrated across all three research pillars. Measurable target: Registry supporting ≥30 external tools/APIs across all demonstrator domains; tool invocation success rate ≥95%; mean recovery time from tool failure ≤5 seconds; full provenance traces for 100% of invocations, by month 36.

OBJ-4 — Healthcare demonstrator — clinical decision support (lead: Nordic Institute of Health Sciences, NIHS): Validate the integrated MAESTRO framework in a clinical decision support system that assists clinicians in diagnostic reasoning by autonomously gathering patient data via HL7 FHIR integration, planning differential diagnoses, and coordinating specialist sub-agents. Measurable target: Diagnostic reasoning accuracy within 10% of specialist clinician performance on a benchmark of 500 de-identified clinical cases spanning 5 diagnostic domains, validated by clinical experts at NIHS, by month 42.

OBJ-5 — Manufacturing demonstrator — process optimisation (lead: Istituto Superiore di Ingegneria Avanzata, ISIA): Validate the integrated MAESTRO framework in a multi-agent system that monitors, diagnoses, and optimises manufacturing processes in real time across production line segments at ISIA's Industry 4.0 pilot factory. Measurable target: ≥15% reduction in defect rates and ≥10% reduction in energy consumption compared to baseline operations, validated over a 3-month operational period, by month 42.

OBJ-6 — Logistics transfer demonstrator — cross-sector generalisation (lead: EuroLog International AG, ELI): Validate the generalisability and portability of the integrated MAESTRO framework through a logistics and supply chain demonstrator coordinating operations across multiple actors, re-planning in response to disruptions, and maintaining consistent state across distributed supply chains. Measurable target: ≥20% improvement in disruption recovery time and ≥15% improvement in delivery schedule adherence over current planning systems, validated on ELI operational scenarios across 3 supply chain corridors, by month 42.

Beyond the state of the art, MAESTRO makes three architecturally grounded advances that are not achievable from current independent research threads. First, the neuro-symbolic planning engine (Pillar 1) provides formal guarantees that pure LLM-based approaches cannot offer: verifiable sub-task decomposition with formally specified pre-conditions and post-conditions, integration of search-based algorithms (MCTS, HTN) with LLM-driven heuristic evaluation, and plan monitoring with provable recovery from verified intermediate states — directly addressing the fragility and degradation of LLM chain-of-thought planning over long task horizons. Second, the multi-agent coordination layer (Pillar 3) develops formally specified delegation and negotiation protocols with convergence guarantees and auditable delegation traces, distinguishing MAESTRO from ad-hoc LLM-based agent communication patterns that lack formal convergence properties and accountability. Third, the External Tool and API Orchestration Layer is architecturally central across all three pillars — the planning engine decides when and which tools to invoke; the memory architecture stores tool capabilities, execution histories, and learned selection heuristics; and the multi-agent coordination layer governs shared tool access and resolves conflicts when multiple agents require the same external resource — addressing SR-04 as a genuine architectural research contribution rather than a bolt-on function-calling interface.

MAESTRO operates from approximately TRL 2 at project start — developing new agent architecture concepts from first principles, with novel planning algorithms, memory systems, and coordination protocols — and advances to TRL 5 by project end, with validation in operational environments across two call-identified Apply AI sectors (healthcare at NIHS; advanced manufacturing at ISIA) and one cross-sector transfer demonstrator (logistics at ELI). This Research and Innovation Action trajectory from foundational research to operational validation is consistent with the call's focus on advancing next-generation agent capabilities while demonstrating readiness for Apply AI sector deployment.

sub_sections.title: Methodology
sub_sections.content:
Overall methodology and concepts: MAESTRO's methodology is organised around the co-design thesis that its three research pillars and the External Tool and API Orchestration Layer must share common data representations, evaluation benchmarks, and integration interfaces from project start. This integration-first design is enforced architecturally through WP8 (Integration, Evaluation and Benchmarking, lead Fundación Ibérica de Innovación Tecnológica, FIIT), which defines the cross-cutting evaluation framework (T8-01: cross-cutting evaluation framework and KPI definition) and leads unified framework integration (T8-02: MAESTRO unified framework integration) by synthesising outputs from WP2 (planning engine software prototype D2-02, month 36), WP3 (adaptive memory system software D3-02, month 30), and WP4 (decentralised coordination software D4-02, month 36). Progress is assessed through structured 6-month integration checkpoints and standardised benchmarking across all three demonstrator domains.

Pillar 1 — Neuro-Symbolic Planning and Reasoning Engine (WP2, lead ATU; contributing CERIA, BIIS, Boreal AI Labs, BAL): The planning engine is developed through three tasks. T2-01 (sub-task graph decomposition design with formal pre/post-conditions) establishes the formal sub-task graph structure and verification checkpoint architecture. T2-02 (MCTS and HTN planning integration with LLM heuristics) develops the hybrid search mechanism that uses LLM-generated heuristics to guide symbolic search, exploiting the complementary strengths of neural flexibility and symbolic rigor. T2-03 (plan monitoring and self-correction implementation) implements runtime plan monitoring that detects execution failures and triggers replanning from the most recently verified intermediate state, overcoming the fragility of chain-of-thought planning over long task horizons. Key deliverables: neuro-symbolic planning architecture specification (D2-01, month 18) and planning engine software prototype (D2-02, month 36). The assumptions underpinning Pillar 1 are that LLM-based heuristics provide useful but noisy guidance for symbolic search, and that formal verification at plan boundary checkpoints — rather than at every reasoning step — provides tractable guarantees without prohibitive computational cost.

Pillar 2 — Adaptive Memory Architecture (WP3, lead BIIS; contributing ATU, CERIA, BAL): T3-01 (tri-store memory architecture design) defines the three memory components — working memory (structured, attention-managed short-term store for current goals, active plan state, and recent observations), episodic memory (retrieval-augmented store of past task episodes indexed by context similarity enabling experience-based behaviour without retraining), and semantic memory (persistent knowledge graph connecting domain concepts, tool capabilities, and task patterns) — and their interaction interfaces. T3-02 (memory indexing and retrieval strategy implementation) develops the indexing and retrieval mechanisms for efficient episodic and semantic recall. T3-03 (forgetting mechanism and coherence maintenance) implements the memory controller's decisions about what to commit to long-term memory, what to retrieve, and when to forget, balancing recall accuracy against computational cost. Key deliverables: memory architecture design specification (D3-01, month 12) and adaptive memory system software (D3-02, month 30).

Pillar 3 — Decentralised Multi-Agent Coordination (WP4, lead CERIA; contributing ATU, BIIS): T4-01 (task-delegation and negotiation protocol design) defines the formal protocol enabling agents to decompose tasks, negotiate subtask assignments, and establish shared commitment structures with provable convergence. T4-02 (convergence and conflict resolution under partial observability) addresses scenarios where agent plans interfere, resources are contested, or partial observations produce inconsistent world models, developing convergence proofs and conflict resolution mechanisms grounded in game theory and mechanism design — CERIA's primary expertise, demonstrated through its ERC Advanced Grant on formal methods for multi-agent systems. T4-03 (auditable multi-agent interaction trace module) implements structured delegation traces that human operators can inspect and audit. Key deliverables: multi-agent coordination protocol specification (D4-01, month 18) and decentralised coordination software (D4-02, month 36).

Cross-cutting — External Tool and API Orchestration Layer: The orchestration layer spans all three pillars and develops: (1) a typed tool registry with machine-readable capability models (input/output schemas, preconditions, postconditions, cost and reliability metadata) that agents can query and update; (2) formal tool selection and invocation policies integrated with Pillar 1's planning engine, determining when and which tools to invoke based on task requirements, expected accuracy gains, and cost/latency trade-offs; (3) standardised API adapters abstracting heterogeneous external APIs — REST, GraphQL, and domain-specific protocols including HL7 FHIR in the healthcare demonstrator and OPC-UA in the manufacturing demonstrator — into a uniform invocation interface; (4) post-invocation verification mechanisms validating tool outputs against expected schemas and domain constraints before incorporating results into agent state, grounding agent reasoning in verified external data; (5) execution monitoring with timeout detection, error classification, and automated fallback and recovery integrated with Pillar 1's self-correction capabilities; and (6) auditable tool execution traces stored in Pillar 2's adaptive memory for experience-based tool selection and in Pillar 3's coordination layer for delegation accountability.

Important challenges and mitigations: (1) Integration complexity across three pillars is mitigated by the co-design approach enforcing shared interfaces from project start, with WP8's T8-02 providing the dedicated integration milestone. (2) Computational cost of formal verification at scale is mitigated by selective deployment of formal verification at plan boundary checkpoints rather than at every reasoning step, with LLM heuristics handling intermediate steps. (3) Clinical validation constraints are mitigated by NIHS's established clinical research ethics infrastructure and access to a de-identified clinical data repository of over 3 million ethics-approved patient records and a clinical simulation environment enabling pre-deployment validation before live clinical interaction. (4) Manufacturing operational environment complexity is mitigated by ISIA's Industry 4.0 pilot factory with sensor-instrumented production lines and digital twin simulation environment, enabling controlled pre-validation before live operational testing.

National and international R&I activities feeding into the project: MAESTRO explicitly builds on prior results from Horizon Europe and H2020 projects undertaken by consortium members. ATU contributes experience from an H2020 project on trustworthy AI (serving as WP lead for hybrid AI integration) and an HE project contributing to the AI-on-demand platform. BIIS builds on its H2020 project on knowledge infrastructure for digital research and HE project on unified transformers for text and retrieval. CERIA grounds Pillar 3 in its H2020 project on autonomous multi-agent exploration and ERC Advanced Grant on formal methods for multi-agent systems. NIHS brings experience from its HE project on European Health Data Space pilot implementation and H2020 project on AI for clinical imaging diagnostics. ISIA contributes from its HE project on AI for manufacturing process optimisation and H2020 project on big data for smart factory environments. BAL draws on its HE project on European large language models and EuroHPC use for large-scale LLM training. FIIT builds on its H2020 contribution to the AI-on-demand platform and HE project on AI testing and evaluation frameworks. These prior results provide the technical foundations from which MAESTRO's unified architecture advances.

Interdisciplinary approach: MAESTRO is intrinsically interdisciplinary. Pillar 1 synthesises automated planning, formal methods, and large language model research — bringing together communities that have historically operated in isolation. Pillar 2 integrates neural information retrieval, knowledge representation and reasoning, and cognitive science-inspired memory models. Pillar 3 draws on game theory and mechanism design alongside multi-agent systems and distributed computing. The External Tool and API Orchestration Layer requires software engineering, API design, and domain-specific protocol expertise. The three demonstrators bring clinical informatics (NIHS, lead WP5), industrial engineering and smart manufacturing (ISIA, lead WP6), and logistics and supply chain management (ELI, lead WP7) into the research design, ensuring that the methodology is validated against real disciplinary constraints in each sector. The co-design of these disciplines is enabled by WP8's cross-cutting evaluation framework, which establishes common KPI definitions and benchmarking standards applicable across all demonstrator domains. Social science and humanities (SSH) contributions are embedded primarily through the clinical informatics expertise of NIHS — encompassing AI ethics in healthcare applications — and through FIIT's responsible AI assessment and certification expertise, which frames the evaluation of agent behaviour against trustworthiness and accountability criteria.

Gender dimension in research and innovation content: The gender dimension in MAESTRO's research content is most directly relevant in the healthcare demonstrator (WP5, lead NIHS). Clinical decision support systems trained and validated on healthcare datasets must account for documented sex and gender differences in diagnostic patterns, disease presentation, and treatment response; the clinical benchmark design (T5-01: clinical use case definition and diagnostic benchmark design) will incorporate sex-disaggregated performance evaluation across the 500-case diagnostic benchmark spanning 5 diagnostic domains. In the broader agent framework evaluation (WP8, lead FIIT), the cross-cutting evaluation framework (T8-01) will examine whether LLM-derived planning heuristics and memory retrieval patterns exhibit gender-related biases and document mitigation measures. Consortium gender diversity and team composition are monitored as part of WP1 quality assurance (T1-02: quality assurance and risk monitoring). Note: specific gender equality plan commitments and detailed sex/gender analysis protocols will be developed in accordance with the Gendered Innovations approach during WP5 T5-01 and WP8 T8-01; some elements of the gender dimension implementation described here are inferred from the disciplinary expertise of consortium members (NIHS's AI ethics in healthcare; FIIT's responsible AI assessment) rather than explicitly specified at the concept note level in the project brief.

Open science practices: MAESTRO adopts an open-by-default posture across all research outputs. All core software outputs — the neuro-symbolic planning engine, the adaptive memory system, the decentralised coordination software, and the External Tool and API Orchestration Layer — will be released as open-source software under permissive licences (D9-02: open-source software release, month 36, lead Boreal AI Labs, BAL), enabling reuse by the European AI research community, SMEs, and startups. All communicable research outputs — frameworks, models, benchmarks, and demonstrator results — will be shared with the European R&D community through the AI-on-demand platform (OBJ-7; SR-10), contributing to European AI research infrastructure. Benchmark datasets generated in WP8 (D8-02: MAESTRO benchmark suite dataset, month 42) will be publicly released to enable community comparison and replication. Demonstrator results will be validated in Testing and Experiment Facilities (TEFs) through FIIT's TEF access and disseminated through FIIT's EDIH network across 6 EU member states. The project links to AI Factories and Data Labs — including BAL's EuroHPC pre-exascale supercomputer access, ATU's GPU cluster of 128 A100 GPUs, and national supercomputing centre access at BIIS — for compute-intensive training and evaluation. Citizen and end-user engagement occurs through clinical expert validation in WP5 and through EDIH-mediated dissemination to SMEs and industry stakeholders across sectors.

Data management plan: A Data Management Plan will be produced as a distinct deliverable in WP1 within the first 6 months (D1-02: Data management plan, month 6, lead ATU), ensuring all research data is findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable (FAIR). The plan will address privacy-preserving handling of clinical data at NIHS (de-identified datasets under established ethics approval covering over 3 million patient records), industrial operational data at ISIA and ELI (anonymised shipment and production records), and public benchmark datasets generated in WP8, including access conditions and licensing for all publicly released artifacts.

claim_statuses.claim_summary: Call topic HORIZON-CL4-2026-05-DIGITAL-EMERGING-02 scope coverage across SR-01 through SR-11
OBJ-1: ≥40% improvement in plan success rate over LLM baselines (ReAct, Reflexion) on WebArena/SWE-bench/GAIA for tasks >10 steps, by month 36, lead ATU
OBJ-2: ≥30% improvement in task coherence over context-window-only baselines for interactions >50 agent steps, by month 30, lead BIIS
OBJ-3: coordination of ≥5 heterogeneous agents with ≥25% improvement in joint task completion and formal convergence proof, by month 36, lead CERIA
OBJ-8: External Tool and API Orchestration Layer — ≥30 tools/APIs, ≥95% invocation success, ≤5s recovery, 100% provenance traces, by month 36, lead ATU
OBJ-4: Healthcare demonstrator — diagnostic accuracy within 10% of clinician performance on 500 de-identified clinical cases, 5 domains, NIHS, by month 42
OBJ-5: Manufacturing demonstrator — ≥15% defect reduction, ≥10% energy reduction at ISIA pilot factory, 3-month operational period, by month 42
OBJ-6: Logistics demonstrator — ≥20% disruption recovery improvement, ≥15% schedule adherence improvement, 3 ELI supply chain corridors, by month 42
TRL 5 at project end, validation in operational environments in two call-identified Apply AI sectors
